examples.title: Checking Universal Quantifier Truth
examples.description: Algorithm to check if ∀x P(x) is true for a finite domain
examples.explanation: This algorithm checks if a universally quantified statement is true by testing the predicate P(x) for every element in the domain. If any element fails (returns false), the universal statement is false. If all pass, it's true. For infinite domains, this algorithm would not terminate.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is size of domain

examples.title: Checking Existential Quantifier Truth
examples.description: Algorithm to check if ∃x P(x) is true for a finite domain
examples.explanation: This algorithm checks if an existential quantified statement is true by searching for at least one element in the domain that satisfies the predicate P(x). It returns true as soon as it finds one, making it efficient.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case, O(1) best case

examples.title: Negating Quantifiers Programmatically
examples.description: Shows how to negate quantified statements in code
examples.explanation: Demonstrates De Morgan's laws for quantifiers in algorithmic form. To negate a universal quantifier, we check for at least one counterexample. To negate an existential quantifier, we verify that no element satisfies the predicate.

examples.title: Nested Quantifier Evaluation
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate ∀x ∃y P(x,y) for finite domains
examples.code:
// Evaluate ∀x ∃y P(x,y)
// Returns true if for every x, there exists some y such that P(x,y) is true

FUNCTION EvaluateForAllExists(X_domain, Y_domain, P):
 FOR each x IN X_domain:
 found_y = false
 FOR each y IN Y_domain:
 IF P(x, y) THEN:
 found_y = true
 BREAK // Found a y for this x
 END IF
 END FOR
 IF NOT found_y THEN:
 RETURN false // No y works for this x
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN true

// Note: ∃y ∀x P(x,y) would have the loops reversed:
// First find a single y, then check if it works for ALL x
examples.explanation: Shows how to evaluate nested quantifiers with different orders. For ∀x ∃y, we iterate through each x and find a suitable y for each. For ∃y ∀x, we would try each y and check if it works for all x. The different order can lead to different results.
